evaluation.manager_summary: Jason called about cocaine treatment with a confidentiality concern regarding employer knowledge. Nina handled the privacy objection expertly with clear HIPAA framing and captured full insurance details and clinical screening (cocaine use escalating over 2 years, polysubstance with alcohol, stress-triggered relapse pattern). The call failed on next-step clarity — Nina gave only vague follow-up language with no specific timing or ownership, forcing Jason to ask 'when?'

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent used the caller's name and responded to concerns directly, but opened with a functional question ('What's on your mind?') without emotional acknowledgement. The tone was professional but somewhat transactional, lacking warmth in the initial 60 seconds.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: In the first moments, acknowledge the courage it takes to call before moving to 'what's on your mind' — build rapport before information gathering.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent captured carrier name (BluePeak Health), member ID, and group number. Verification was framed supportively ('when we verify your benefits, that call goes through our intake team, not back to your employer's HR. You're safe.'). No coverage guarantees made before verification.

Agent captured substance type (cocaine), duration (two years), escalation pattern, financial impact, and polysubstance use (alcohol). Asked about prior attempts to stop and explored stress triggers. Stayed within intake role without diagnosing.

No explicit urgency signals were present (no withdrawal, suicidal ideation, or immediate danger). Agent did not probe for urgency indicators or establish a timeline for when treatment should begin. Adequate but unremarkable.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: When you hear escalating use and financial impact, ask when they last used and whether they're experiencing any physical or mental health concerns that need immediate attention.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent handled the employer confidentiality objection exemplarily: acknowledged it as common, provided specific HIPAA framing, explained how insurance claims work, and offered concrete guidance on FMLA if needed. No defensive or salesy language.

Agent provided only vague language ('We'll follow up', 'We'll be in touch soon', 'Keep an eye on your phone'). No specific timing, no ownership established, no concrete action step set. Caller had to prompt for timing.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Always set a specific next step with a clear timeline and owner before ending the call — e.g., 'Our benefits team will call you tomorrow by 2 PM to review your coverage and schedule your first appointment.'

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent maintained perfect boundary discipline. No diagnostic language, no outcome promises, no scope overreach. Clinical topics (stress triggers, counsellor work) were framed appropriately. Insurance verification explicitly deferred ('when we verify your benefits').

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Of course, Jason. What's on your mind?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: That's a really common concern and I want to give you an honest answer.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I appreciate you sharing that. Two years of use with escalating frequency and financial impact — those are meaningful signals.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Yes. BluePeak Health. ID BP41089959, group GRP-623482.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: when we verify your benefits, that call goes through our intake team, not back to your employer's HR. You're safe.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I've been using cocaine on and off for about two years. Lately more on. I'm spending money I don't have. My partner is frustrated.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Jason, are you also drinking or using anything else alongside the cocaine?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: And are there times you've tried to stop on your own?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Lately more on. I'm spending money I don't have.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I've tried. I go a few days and then something stressful happens and I'm back.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'm worried that if I use my insurance for this, my employer will find out. I can't have that.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: That's a really common concern and I want to give you an honest answer. Your insurance claim is private — your employer receives aggregate data for cost management but does not receive information abo

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: And if you decide FMLA is something you want to explore — which some people do to protect their job during treatment — we can connect you with guidance on that.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: We'll follow up with you about your options.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: We'll be in touch soon. Keep an eye on your phone.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Under HIPAA, your treatment records are protected.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: That cycle — the stress trigger leading back — is something our counsellors work on specifically, especially in IOP.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: when we verify your benefits, that call goes through our intake team
